Q1: Honestly, my study routine was pretty inconsistent this semester. I'd try to set aside time in the evenings, maybe around 24 hours a week total, but it rarely went according to plan. I'm a visual learner, so I'd often spend time making diagrams and charts from the material, which took longer than just reading. With limited resources at home, I had to be strategic about when I could access things online versus what I could work with offline.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied a lot depending on what was due and what else was going on in my life. I have extracurriculars that take up significant time, so some weeks I'd barely touch the course material until a deadline loomed. Other weeks, when things were calmer, I'd try to get ahead, but that motivation was hard to maintain consistently. Deadlines were really the main driver of when I buckled down.
Q3: Usually I logged into the LMS when I needed to check if something was due or to grab materials for an assignment. Sometimes I'd get an email notification about a discussion post or announcement and that would prompt me to log in. It wasn't really a habit I enjoyed—more like something I did out of necessity when I remembered or when pressure built up.
Q4: There were brief periods where logging in felt more routine, especially early in the semester when I was trying to stay on top of my seven online courses. But honestly, that didn't last long. The routine broke down when stress from other areas of life crept in, and then logging in became more sporadic. I never really hit a rhythm where it felt natural.
Q5: The video content was probably the most useful for me because I'm a visual learner and seeing concepts explained helped them stick better than just reading. I watched quite a bit of video content when I could. The discussion forums were also helpful sometimes because seeing how other students interpreted things gave me new perspectives on material I was struggling with.
Q6: I definitely ignored a lot of the reading materials and some of the supplementary resources. With low resources and limited time, I had to prioritize what seemed most directly connected to assignments and exams. If something looked optional or like it would take too long to get through, I'd skip it or tell myself I'd come back to it later, which usually didn't happen.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time if I could clearly see how it connected to getting a better grade or understanding something I was confused about. If it was going to help me on an assignment or the exam, I'd engage with it. But if it seemed tangential or like busywork, my motivation—which is already pretty low—just wasn't there to push through it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like luxuries I couldn't afford, honestly. With required work already piling up across seven courses and my extracurriculars, optional stuff got pushed to the bottom of the list. Required activities had that external pressure that forced me to engage even when I didn't feel like it, but optional ones needed internal motivation I often couldn't muster.
Q9: Absolutely, a lot of my studying happened offline. I'd download videos and PDFs when I had good internet access, then study from those later without being connected. I also made my own visual notes and diagrams that I'd review repeatedly. My textbook and handwritten notes probably got more attention than the LMS would ever show.
Q10: There were many times I'd be reviewing my printed notes or working through problems on paper where the LMS had no idea I was engaged. I'd also discuss concepts with classmates in person during my extracurricular activities, which was actually really helpful. Those study sessions and conversations were invisible to the system but genuinely productive for my learning.
Q11: Yeah, definitely. I'd often open a video or document once, download it or take detailed notes, and then never access it through the LMS again. My internet access isn't always reliable, so it made sense to grab what I needed and work with it offline. The LMS would show one view, but I might have reviewed that material five or six times on my own.
Q12: Mid-semester was really tough. The stress was building up—I'd rate it pretty high—and managing seven online courses while keeping up with extracurriculars felt overwhelming. There were weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to engage deeply with anything. The CBT sessions I had actually helped me get through some of that, which is why I'm feeling happier now.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or stressed, the LMS was the first thing I'd neglect. I'd maybe log in quickly to check deadlines but wouldn't actually engage meaningfully with the content. Competing responsibilities from my activities and just general life stuff with limited resources meant the LMS often got the minimum effort I could get away with.
Q14: Oh, for sure. There were times I'd have videos playing while my mind was completely elsewhere, or I'd scroll through materials without really absorbing anything. I was technically 'active' but not actually learning. Those sessions felt hollow, like I was going through the motions to feel like I was doing something productive.
Q15: I postponed things constantly, especially when they felt overwhelming or when I wasn't sure how to start. Even knowing something was important, if it triggered that anxious feeling of not knowing where to begin, I'd push it off. My resilience isn't great, so once I started avoiding something, it was hard to break that cycle until the deadline forced my hand.
Q16: Deadlines were basically my entire motivation system. I'd see a deadline approaching and suddenly find the energy to log in, watch videos, and complete work. Without that external pressure, I'd drift. My LMS activity probably spiked dramatically in the days before things were due and was pretty quiet otherwise.
Q17: Feedback had a mixed effect on me. When I got positive feedback or good scores on assignments, it boosted my mood and made me more likely to engage. But if feedback was critical or I did poorly, it sometimes made me want to avoid the course for a while. My assignment scores were actually pretty good, which helped keep me going.
Q18: The course structure was okay, but having seven online courses meant I was constantly juggling. When modules were clearly organized and I could see exactly what was expected, I engaged more easily. When things were vague or spread across multiple places, I'd get frustrated and disengage. Clear visual organization really helped someone like me.
Q19: Having video content made engagement easier because of my learning style. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks or when the platform felt cluttered. Also, discussion requirements felt forced sometimes—I participated, but often just to meet the minimum rather than because I found it genuinely valuable.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is anxious or uncertain about what to submit. They might be exploring, trying to understand what's expected, or second-guessing their work. Or maybe they're dealing with perfectionism or fear of failure. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just spinning wheels.
Q21: That actually sounds a lot like me in some ways. My exam score was decent and I passed, but my LMS activity was pretty low. I did a lot of learning offline with downloaded materials and my own notes. Someone looking at my login frequency wouldn't see the hours I spent studying away from the platform.
Q22: Course logs can show patterns like when someone logs in and what they click on, which might indicate deadline-driven behavior or consistent habits. But they miss the quality of engagement, offline studying, emotional state, and whether someone actually understood the material. They're measuring presence, not learning.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about understanding the material, not just completing tasks. It's when I'm curious, asking questions in my head, making connections to other things I know. It's not just about time spent—it's about mental investment. Some of my most engaged moments happened while staring at my handwritten notes, not at a screen.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I went through a really stressful period and got help through CBT sessions that improved my mental state. It wouldn't show the peer conversations during my extracurriculars where I learned a lot, or the hours spent with downloaded materials. It also can't capture that despite low motivation, I genuinely tried to understand the material in my own way.
